Criteria Description: Models are effectively independent. Two separate projects glued together without interaction.
Naive combination (e.g., simple averaging of predictions) without justification for why this specific combination makes sense.
Linear flow only (Model A output -> Model B input). No feedback loop or complex interaction. The DL model adds little value over the ML baseline.
Logical hybrid architecture. The combination addresses a specific weakness of one model (e.g., using a Bayesian Net to explain LLM uncertainty).
Strong coupling. The system leverages the strengths of both (e.g., Neural Feature extraction feeding a symbolic reasoning engine). Performance improves noticeably.
The DL model significantly and logically improves upon the ML baseline. The interaction is symbiotic (e.g., Neuro-symbolic or Differentiable Programming). The whole is greater than the sum of parts.
No comparison or ablation attempted.
Compares the Hybrid model against a weak or un-tuned baseline to artificially inflate success.
Mentions other models but does not provide concrete metrics comparing them on the same test set.
Clear table present showing results for ML-only, DL-only, and Hybrid. Metrics are consistent.
Discusses the table results. Explains which specific component contributed the most to the performance gain.
Deep analysis showing exactly WHAT happens when the DL or ML part is removed (e.g., "Removing the LSTM caused accuracy on long sequences to drop by 20%"). proves necessity of complexity.
No diagram provided.
Low-resolution screenshot, hand-drawn sketch, or illegible text.
Generic block diagram (e.g., boxes labeled just "Model" or "Input") that could apply to any project.
High-level visual showing data flow between probabilistic and neural models. Direction of arrows is correct.
Includes shapes of data tensors (e.g., (Batch, 256, 256)) and specific layer names. clear distinction between the ML and DL components.
Professional-grade. Uses standard notation. Visualizes the specific fusion mechanism (e.g., concatenation vs multiplication). Aesthetically suitable for a conference paper.
Code does not run. Missing files or syntax errors.
Hardcoded paths (e.g., "/Users/Name/Desktop..."). No requirements file.
Code runs but requires significant debugging by the grader. Poor variable naming or structure.
Clean GitHub repo with requirements.txt. Code follows a logical structure and runs with standard commands.
Includes a Readme with setup instructions. Code is commented. Environment is reproducible.
Fully reproducible pipeline. Dockerized or includes a setup script. Follows standard conference paper formatting for the report.
No additional features implemented.
Attempted an extra feature but it is non-functional or trivial (e.g., simple color change in a plot).
One extra feature implemented, but it is buggy or barely adds value.
Well thought out implementation of 1 significant extra parameter (e.g., a functional Web UI or Deployment).
Well thought out implementation of 2 significant extra parameters (e.g., UI + CI/CD Pipeline).
Well thought out implementation of 3 or more extra mile parameters, OR one massive research-grade addition (e.g., writing a custom CUDA kernel or novel theoretical proof).
